antiPatterns: Prioritizing predator reproductive-efficiency traits while nutrients are critically low.
Ignoring the disconnect between the full buffer and the empty nutrient pool.
Focusing on biomass growth of consumers when the foundation (nutrients) is failing.

directions.acceptanceSignal: Fox population growth rate slows down significantly.
directions.avoid: Traits that increase predator energy gain or individual survival.
directions.expectedGardenEffect: Stabilization of the fox population growth rate.
directions.label: A
directions.title: Fox Population Reproductive Cap
directions.why: The current population size is over-taxing the ecosystem and cannot be sustained by existing nutrient generation.

directions.avoid: Traits that increase nutrient consumption rates.
directions.expectedGardenEffect: Steady increase in active nutrient pool.
directions.label: B
directions.title: Adaptive Nutrient Buffer Transduction
directions.why: The buffer is full, but active nutrients are critically low; the system requires a mechanism to bypass inefficient conversion.

directions.acceptanceSignal: Improved nutrient regeneration rate observed in population snapshots.
directions.avoid: Traits that increase beetle predation pressure.
directions.expectedGardenEffect: Enhanced nutrient regeneration rate from decomposers.
directions.label: C
directions.title: Fungal-Beetle Nutrient Synergy
directions.why: The fungal-beetle pathway is the primary natural conduit for energy back into the nutrient pool.

directions.avoid: Traits that increase beetle vulnerability or predation risk.
directions.expectedGardenEffect: Resilient, stable beetle population despite high predator density.
directions.label: D
directions.title: Beetle Population Protective Resilience
directions.why: The prey base is the only thing preventing total fox collapse, and it remains vulnerable to over-predation.

results.evidence: Active nutrient levels remain critically low.
results.label: C

review.summary: The fox population continued its explosive growth (3434 to 4689), ignoring metabolic constraints and exacerbating the critical nutrient shortage (dropped from 9 to 6).

thesis: The fox population runaway (4689) is causing a critical nutrient crisis (6). We must transition from optimizing individual metabolic efficiency—which has ironically fueled expansion—to enforcing structural population caps and aggressive nutrient-conversion throughput to restore the active nutrient pool from the 100-value buffer.
